summary: DHH comments on a benchmark comparison between Macs and PCs for digital photography, noting the unsurprising result that PCs are faster and cheaper. He argues the real value of the comparison isn't the results themselves but that it forces an explicit discussion about what performance actually means and how much it matters. He suggests most people struggle to properly evaluate these questions.
key_points: PC vs Mac speed benchmarks confirm what most already know: PCs are faster and cheaper
The real value of benchmarks is forcing explicit debate about the meaning and importance of performance
Most people struggle to evaluate what performance means and how much it actually matters
money_quotes: Anyone with half an eye on Apple knows this.
What does performance mean and how much does it matter?
These are fundamental questions that a lot of people are having a hard time evaluating the answers for.
key_insight: Raw performance benchmarks matter less than understanding what performance actually means in context and how much it truly matters for your work.
